title: You get to keep your job
summary: Matthew Berman video discussing job security amid AI developments. The title "You get to keep your job" suggests an optimistic or reassuring take on employment prospects despite AI expansion. The content appears to be a commentary or analysis piece rather than a technical tutorial or tool announcement.
writeup: Sam Altman and Dario Amodei have walked back earlier predictions of a white-collar jobs apocalypse, and this video argues they were right to do so: the layoffs attributed to AI are better explained by post-zero-interest-rate bloat, not automation displacement. The through-line is Jevons' paradox applied to AI, where cheaper inference creates more demand and more jobs rather than fewer, a claim backed by Apollo Research's finding of zero evidence of AI-caused job losses and rising ADP payroll numbers. The deeper problem the video identifies is that the majority of companies are spending heavily on frontier models (Claude Opus 4.8 at $25/M output tokens, Sonnet 4.6 at $15/M) without knowing how to extract proportional real-world output, while a tiny cohort of practitioners is building at a qualitatively different scale.
key_points: Sam Altman admitted he was 'pretty wrong' about AI's economic impact, reversing June 2025 warnings about entry-level roles, and his own attempt to delegate Slack and email to AI ended with him returning to manual responses.

Apollo Research, a payroll and economics firm, found zero evidence of AI-related job losses; US ADP weekly employment numbers have been increasing in parallel with rising AI spend since late 2024 when frontier model capability jumped.

Jevons' paradox explains the pattern: as AI inference gets cheaper, companies find previously unjustifiable use cases become viable, expanding total labor demand rather than contracting it, with humans still required for prompting and verification.

Uber burned through its entire 2026 AI budget in four months without a clear payoff in consumer features, and an unidentified company accidentally spent $500 million in tokens in one month, illustrating that high spend does not automatically translate to business output.

Frontier model pricing ranges from $0.87/M output tokens for DeepSeek to $15/M for Claude Sonnet 4.6 to $25/M for Claude Opus 4.8, and the video argues most production use cases do not require frontier capability, meaning cost curves will improve for the majority of deployments.

Peter Steinberger, creator of OpenClaw, spent roughly $1.3 million in tokens in one month not to produce code directly but to build what the video calls a 'software factory,' a qualitatively different investment thesis that only a few hundred practitioners worldwide currently operate at.
